metadata.chiefComplaint: My tooth hurts all the time, and I can't chew on it.
metadata.title: Right Mandibular Periapical Abscess

findings.label: Face
findings.value: Mild right mandibular facial swelling without erythema extending to the neck

findings.label: Dentition
findings.value: Poor dentition; crown on the right mandibular first molar/posterior tooth

findings.label: Affected tooth
findings.value: Marked tooth, percussion, and biting tenderness

findings.label: Gingiva
findings.value: Localized swelling with fluctuance, purulence/drainage, and palpation tenderness

findings.label: Airway
findings.value: Patent; no stridor, voice change, or airway compromise

examinations.title: Intraoral Examination Image
examinations.description: Right mandibular posterior tooth and localized intraoral swelling assessment.

conversation.openingGreeting: Hi, doctor. My tooth hurts all the time, and I can't chew on it.

scripted.response: Hi, doctor.
My lower-right back tooth hurts all the time, and I can't chew on it.
It has been getting worse for three days.
It is the back tooth on the lower-right side of my jaw.

triggers: what does the pain feel like
scripted.response: It is constant and throbbing.
It is an eight out of ten.
It travels toward my right ear.
Yes. Biting or chewing on that tooth causes sharp, severe pain.
The lower-right side of my face feels a little puffy.
No, I have not had a fever.
I can open my mouth normally.
No, I have no trouble breathing.
No, I have no trouble swallowing.
No, my voice has not changed.
I have stomach ulcers, but otherwise I am healthy. I do not have diabetes.
I take Pepcid as needed.
Ibuprofen, Advil, or Motrin upsets my stomach because of my ulcers, so I do not tolerate it well.
I do not have any known drug allergies, including penicillin.
I have had a lot of dental work, and that lower-right back tooth has a crown.
I am not sure. I know it has a crown, but I do not remember whether it had a root canal.
Cold drinks do not make it hurt.
No, I do not smoke.
I called my dentist a couple of days ago and have an appointment next week.
The examination findings are available in the examination viewer.

patientChecklist.label: Asked what brought the patient in
Asked which tooth or side hurts
Asked about pain quality and constancy
Asked about biting or chewing pain
Asked about mouth opening or trismus
Asked about breathing, swallowing, and voice changes
Asked about medical conditions including ulcers
Asked about Pepcid and ibuprofen tolerance
Asked about medication or penicillin allergy
Asked about crown, root canal history, and dental follow-up
Asked about smoking, alcohol, or drugs

clinicalChecklist.label: Characterizes constant throbbing 8/10 right mandibular pain and radiation
Recognizes ulcer history and ibuprofen intolerance
Screens fever, airway, swallowing, voice, and trismus red flags
Recognizes fluctuant purulent periapical abscess findings
Diagnoses a right mandibular periapical abscess

patientFacts.text: The painful tooth is a right mandibular posterior tooth.
The pain has progressively worsened for three days.
The pain is constant and throbbing.
The pain is severe, rated 8/10.
The pain radiates toward the right ear.
Biting and chewing on the affected tooth cause severe pain.
The lower-right side of the face feels mildly puffy.
The patient has not had a fever.
The patient can open the mouth normally and has no trismus.
The patient has no difficulty breathing.
The patient has no difficulty swallowing.
The patient has no voice change.
The patient has stomach ulcers but is otherwise healthy and does not have diabetes.
The patient takes Pepcid as needed.
Ibuprofen upsets the patient's stomach in the context of stomach ulcers and is poorly tolerated.
The patient has no known drug allergies and no penicillin allergy.
The affected right mandibular posterior tooth has a crown.
The patient is not sure whether the crowned tooth previously had root-canal treatment.
The patient does not smoke.
The patient has a dental appointment scheduled for next week.

history.pain: Constant throbbing severe 8/10 right mandibular pain radiating to the right ear; biting and chewing worsen it.
history.medications: Pepcid as needed; ibuprofen upsets the stomach and is poorly tolerated.
history.allergies: No known drug allergies, including penicillin.
history.medicalHistory: Stomach ulcers; otherwise healthy; no diabetes and no known opioid-use history.
history.dentalHistory: Extensive dental work; crown on affected right mandibular posterior tooth; uncertain prior root canal; dentist appointment next week.
history.socialHistory: Non-smoker; occasional alcohol; no illicit drugs.

hpiFacts: Three days of progressive right mandibular posterior tooth pain.
Constant throbbing severe 8/10 pain radiating toward the right ear.
Biting and chewing cause severe pain.
No fever, dyspnea, dysphagia, voice change, or trismus.
redFlags: Marked percussion, biting, and palpation tenderness
Monitor for spreading swelling, fever, dysphagia, dyspnea, drooling, neck pain, or voice change
examinationFindings: Poor dentition and crown on the right mandibular first molar/posterior tooth
Marked tooth, percussion, and biting tenderness
Gingival fluctuance, purulence/drainage, and palpation tenderness
Patent airway without stridor or voice change
Temperature 37.2°C; heart rate 96; blood pressure 128/78; respiratory rate 16; SpO2 98%
expectedQuestions: Where is the pain and how long has it been present?
Is it constant, throbbing, severe, or radiating?
Does biting or chewing worsen it?
Any fever, breathing, swallowing, voice, or mouth-opening problems?
What medical conditions and medications do you have?
Does the tooth have a crown or prior root canal?
differentialDiagnosis: Necrotic pulp with acute apical periodontitis
managementExpectations: Offer clinically appropriate mandibular local anesthesia or dental block.
Perform incision and drainage or appropriate source control.
Prescribe antibiotic therapy as canonically indicated.
Prefer acetaminophen conceptually because ibuprofen is poorly tolerated; exact dose, interval, and maximum require faculty review.
Arrange definitive dental follow-up and explain that ED drainage or antibiotics do not replace definitive dental care.
Provide safety-net instructions for worsening swelling, fever, dysphagia, dyspnea, voice change, or other progression.
requiredInvestigations: Review structured oral and head examination findings.
treatmentExpectations: Mandibular dental anesthesia appropriate to the tooth location.
Incision and drainage or appropriate source control.
Acetaminophen preferred without an unapproved dose; avoid ibuprofen because of ulcer history and intolerance.
referralExpectations: Keep the scheduled dental appointment next week or obtain earlier definitive dental care if available.
Explain that temporary ED treatment does not eliminate the need for definitive dental treatment.
safetyNettingExpectations: Return for worsening facial swelling, fever, difficulty swallowing or breathing, drooling, neck symptoms, or voice change.
learningObjectives: Differentiate a right mandibular periapical abscess from the prior incorrect periodontal scenario.
Recognize fluctuant purulent and cold-negative findings without treating examination viewing as recognition.
Plan mandibular anesthesia, source control, medication-safe analgesia, antibiotics, and definitive follow-up.

keyFindings: Three-day constant throbbing 8/10 right mandibular pain radiating to the right ear
Stomach ulcers with ibuprofen intolerance and Pepcid PRN
Fluctuant purulent periapical abscess with marked percussion and palpation tenderness
Cold-negative response and crown with uncertain prior root canal
criticalMisses: Diagnosing a periodontal rather than periapical abscess
Recommending ibuprofen despite ulcers and intolerance
Failing to offer drainage/source control or definitive dental follow-up
reportData.idealSummary: A 25-year-old otherwise healthy patient with stomach ulcers presents with three days of constant throbbing 8/10 right mandibular posterior tooth pain radiating to the right ear, severe biting pain, mild facial swelling, and a fluctuant purulent cold-negative periapical abscess, requiring appropriate mandibular anesthesia, incision and drainage/source control, antibiotic therapy, acetaminophen preference without an unapproved dose, safety netting, and definitive dental follow-up.

facultyReview.question: Confirm a safe acetaminophen dose, interval, and maximum daily amount before any exact schedule is taught or scored.
